solution: Reduce the exponent modulo 4: \( [[n]] \equiv [[n]] \pmod 4 \) selects the corresponding entry of the cycle, so \( i^{[[n]]} = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This is the value of \( i^{4k} \); recheck \( [[n]] \bmod 4 \).
This is the value of \( i^{4k+2} \); recheck \( [[n]] \bmod 4 \).
This is the value of \( i^{4k+1} \); recheck \( [[n]] \bmod 4 \).
This is the value of \( i^{4k+3} \); recheck \( [[n]] \bmod 4 \).
A power of \( i \) is never \( 0 \); it always has modulus 1.
A power of \( i \) is always purely real or purely imaginary, never \( 1+i \).
A power of \( i \) is always purely real or purely imaginary, never \( -1-i \).

solution: A negative exponent means \( i^{-[[n]]} = \dfrac{1}{i^{[[n]]}} \); since \( 1/i = -i \), this equals \( i^{-1\cdot[[n]]} = i^{4-([[n]] \bmod 4)} \) when \( [[n]] \not\equiv 0 \pmod 4 \), giving \( i^{-[[n]]} = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This flips the sign of the exponent, giving \( i^{[[n]]} \) instead of \( i^{-[[n]]} \); the two agree only when the cycle position is real.
This is the value \( i^{-[[n]]} \) would take if \( [[n]] \) were a multiple of \( 4 \); recheck \( [[n]] \bmod 4 \).
This is the value \( i^{-[[n]]} \) would take if \( [[n]] \equiv 2 \pmod 4 \); recheck \( [[n]] \bmod 4 \).
This forgets the negative sign introduced by \( 1/i = -i \).
A power of \( i \) is never \( 0 \); it always has modulus 1.
A power of \( i \) is always purely real or purely imaginary, never \( -1+i \).
A power of \( i \) is always purely real or purely imaginary, never \( 1-i \).

stem: Since every 4 consecutive powers of \( i \) sum to \( i + i^2 + i^3 + i^4 = 0 \), evaluate the sum \( i^{1} + i^{2} + i^{3} + \cdots + i^{[[n]]} \).
solution: Group the terms into complete blocks of \( 4 \), each summing to \( 0 \); only the leftover \( [[n]] \bmod 4 \) terms at the end survive, giving \( [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This is just the last term of the sum, \( i^{[[n]]} \), not the running total.
This assumes the sum always collapses to \( 1 \), ignoring the actual remainder block.
This assumes the sum always collapses to \( -1 \), ignoring the actual remainder block.
This assumes the sum always collapses to \( i \), ignoring the actual remainder block.
This is correct only when \( [[n]] \) is a multiple of \( 4 \); otherwise a nonzero remainder block survives.
This confuses the number of terms \( [[n]] \) with the value of the sum.
This is off by one from the correct total.

stem: Let \( i = \sqrt{-1} \). First combine the exponents, then reduce cyclically: evaluate \( i^{[[m]]} \cdot i^{[[n]]} \).
solution: Adding exponents, \( i^{[[m]]}\cdot i^{[[n]]} = i^{[[m]]+[[n]]} \); reducing \( [[m]]+[[n]] \) modulo \( 4 \) gives \( [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This stops after reducing only the first factor, \( i^{[[m]]} \), forgetting to multiply by the second.
This stops after reducing only the second factor, \( i^{[[n]]} \), forgetting to multiply by the first.
This assumes the product always reduces to \( 1 \), ignoring the actual combined exponent.
This assumes the product always reduces to \( -1 \), ignoring the actual combined exponent.
This assumes the product always reduces to \( i \), ignoring the actual combined exponent.
A power of \( i \) is never \( 0 \); it always has modulus 1.
This is off by one from the correct product.

stem: Let \( i = \sqrt{-1} \). First combine the exponents, then reduce cyclically: evaluate \( \dfrac{i^{[[m]]}}{i^{[[n]]}} \).
solution: Subtracting exponents, \( \dfrac{i^{[[m]]}}{i^{[[n]]}} = i^{[[m]]-[[n]]} \); reducing \( [[m]]-[[n]] \) modulo \( 4 \) gives \( [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This is the numerator power \( i^{[[m]]} \) alone, without dividing by \( i^{[[n]]} \).
This is the denominator power \( i^{[[n]]} \) alone, not the quotient.
This assumes the quotient always reduces to \( 1 \), ignoring the actual exponent difference.
This assumes the quotient always reduces to \( -1 \), ignoring the actual exponent difference.
This assumes the quotient always reduces to \( i \), ignoring the actual exponent difference.
A power of \( i \) is never \( 0 \); it always has modulus 1.
This is off by one from the correct quotient.

solution: Reduce the exponent modulo \( 4 \) using the cycle of \( -i \): \( (-i)^{[[n]]} = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This uses the cycle of \( i \) instead of \( -i \); the two cycles agree only at even exponents.
This is the value \( (-i)^{[[n]]} \) would take if \( [[n]] \) were a multiple of \( 4 \); recheck \( [[n]] \bmod 4 \).
This is the value \( (-i)^{[[n]]} \) would take if \( [[n]] \equiv 2 \pmod 4 \); recheck \( [[n]] \bmod 4 \).
This is the value \( (-i)^{[[n]]} \) would take if \( [[n]] \equiv 3 \pmod 4 \); recheck \( [[n]] \bmod 4 \).
This is the value \( (-i)^{[[n]]} \) would take if \( [[n]] \equiv 1 \pmod 4 \); recheck \( [[n]] \bmod 4 \).
A power of \( -i \) is never \( 0 \); it always has modulus 1.
This is off by one from the correct value.
